(4 July 1931 – 2 June 1977), better known by his [stage name](/wiki/Stage_name "Stage name") **Stephen Boyd**, was an actor from [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"). He emerged as a [leading man](/wiki/Leading_man "Leading man") during the late 1950s with his role as the villainous Messala in *[Ben-Hur](/wiki/Ben-Hur_(1959_film) "Ben-Hur (1959 film)")* (1959), a role that earned him the [Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture"). He received his second [Golden Globe](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award") nomination for the musical *[Billy Rose's Jumbo](/wiki/Billy_Rose%27s_Jumbo "Billy Rose's Jumbo")* (1962).

**William Millar** (4 July 1931 – 2 June 1977), better known by his [stage name](/wiki/Stage_name "Stage name") **Stephen Boyd**, was an actor from [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"). He emerged as a [leading man](/wiki/Leading_man "Leading man") during the late 1950s with his role as the villainous Messala in *[Ben-Hur](/wiki/Ben-Hur_(1959_film) "Ben-Hur (1959 film)")* (1959), a role that earned him the [Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture"). He received his second [Golden Globe](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award") nomination for the musical *[Billy Rose's Jumbo](/wiki/Billy_Rose%27s_Jumbo "Billy Rose's Jumbo")* (1962).

Boyd also appeared, sometimes as a hero and sometimes as a malefactor, in the major big-screen productions *[The Man Who Never Was](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Never_Was "The Man Who Never Was")* (1956), *[The Night Heaven Fell](/wiki/The_Night_Heaven_Fell "The Night Heaven Fell")* (1958), *[The Bravados](/wiki/The_Bravados "The Bravados")* (1958), *[The Fall of the Roman Empire](/wiki/The_Fall_of_the_Roman_Empire_(film) "The Fall of the Roman Empire (film)")* (1964), *[Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan_(1965_film) "Genghis Khan (1965 film)")* (1965), *[Fantastic Voyage](/wiki/Fantastic_Voyage "Fantastic Voyage")* (1966), *[The Bible: In the Beginning...](/wiki/The_Bible:_In_the_Beginning... "The Bible: In the Beginning...")* (also 1966) and *[Shalako](/wiki/Shalako_(film) "Shalako (film)")* (1968).

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life")]

Stephen Boyd was born on 4 July 1931 in Whitehouse in [Newtownabbey](/wiki/Newtownabbey "Newtownabbey"), [County Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-bbc-2) He was the youngest of nine children born to [Scots-Irish Canadian](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_Canadians "Scotch-Irish Canadians") parents, James Alexander Millar and his wife Martha Boyd. At a very early age, William, or Billy as he was known, moved with the family to live in [Glengormley](/wiki/Glengormley "Glengormley"). Boyd was raised [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland") and attended the local primary school and [Ballyclare High School](/wiki/Ballyclare_High_School "Ballyclare High School").

At the age of 14, Boyd quit school to work and earn money to help support his family. He eventually joined the [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") Group Theatre, where he learned the behind-the-scenes tasks of the theatre. He became well known in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") for his contributions as a gravel-voiced policeman on *The McCooeys*, the story of a Belfast family written by [Joseph Tomelty](/wiki/Joseph_Tomelty "Joseph Tomelty") that was broadcast on the Northern Irish section of the [BBC Home Service](/wiki/BBC_Home_Service "BBC Home Service").[[3]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-3)[[4]](#cite_note-newsletter.co.uk-4)

Boyd eventually worked his way up to character parts and then starring roles. By nineteen he had toured Canada with summer stock companies. In 1950, he made a coast-to-coast tour of America with the [Clare Tree Major](/wiki/Clare_Tree_Major "Clare Tree Major") Company,[[5]](#cite_note-5) performing *[A Streetcar Named Desire](/wiki/A_Streetcar_Named_Desire "A Streetcar Named Desire")* [[6]](#cite_note-6) in the lead role as [Stanley Kowalski](/wiki/Stanley_Kowalski "Stanley Kowalski"). Boyd later recalled this as "the best performance I ever gave in my life".[[7]](#cite_note-7)

By the time he was 20, Boyd had a wide range of theatre experience, but he longed for the big stage.[[8]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-8) In 1952, he moved to London and worked in a cafeteria and busked outside a cinema in [Leicester Square](/wiki/Leicester_Square "Leicester Square") to get money as he was literally close to starvation.[[3]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-3) Boyd caught his first break as a doorman at the Odeon Theatre.

The Leicester Square Cinema across the street recruited him to usher attendees during the British Academy Awards in the early 1950s. During the awards ceremony, he was noticed by actor [Sir Michael Redgrave](/wiki/Michael_Redgrave "Michael Redgrave"), who used his connections to introduce Boyd to the director of the Windsor Repertory Group.[[8]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-8) At this point, Boyd's stage career in the UK began to flourish with performances in "The Deep Blue Sea" and "Barnett's Folly".[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Early roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early roles")]

Boyd's first role that brought him acclaim[[10]](#cite_note-10) was as a pro-Nazi Irish spy in the movie *[The Man Who Never Was](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Never_Was "The Man Who Never Was")*, based on the book by [Ewen Montagu](/wiki/Ewen_Montagu "Ewen Montagu"). The movie was released in April 1956.

Shortly thereafter, he signed a ten-year contract with [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") studios,[[11]](#cite_note-11) which began prepping him for Hollywood, but it was a while until Boyd actually set foot on a Hollywood back-lot. Boyd's next stop was Portugal, where he acted in *[A Hill in Korea](/wiki/A_Hill_in_Korea "A Hill in Korea")*, which also featured future stars [Michael Caine](/wiki/Michael_Caine "Michael Caine") and [Robert Shaw](/wiki/Robert_Shaw_(actor) "Robert Shaw (actor)").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In June 1956, Boyd was cast in the shipwreck drama *[Abandon Ship!](/wiki/Seven_Waves_Away "Seven Waves Away")* for Columbia Studios starring [Tyrone Power](/wiki/Tyrone_Power "Tyrone Power"). This was filmed in the summer of 1956 in London, where the British Navy built a huge 35,000-gallon water tank for the movie.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In November 1956, for Twentieth Century Fox, Boyd travelled to the [British West Indies](/wiki/British_West_Indies "British West Indies") as part of a large ensemble cast in [Darryl Zanuck](/wiki/Darryl_Zanuck "Darryl Zanuck")'s racially provocative film *[Island in the Sun](/wiki/Island_in_the_Sun_(film) "Island in the Sun (film)")* starring [Dorothy Dandridge](/wiki/Dorothy_Dandridge "Dorothy Dandridge"), based on the [Alec Waugh](/wiki/Alec_Waugh "Alec Waugh") novel.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Boyd portrayed a young English aristocrat who becomes the lover of [Joan Collins](/wiki/Joan_Collins "Joan Collins"). Boyd was loaned out to the [J. Arthur Rank](/wiki/J._Arthur_Rank "J. Arthur Rank") production of *[Seven Thunders](/wiki/Seven_Thunders_(film) "Seven Thunders (film)") (Beast of Marseilles)*, a World War II romance set in Nazi-occupied [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille").[[15]](#cite_note-15) This movie was filmed on location in Marseille and at [Pinewood Studios](/wiki/Pinewood_Studios "Pinewood Studios") in London in the spring of 1957 and featured Boyd in his most prominent starring film role yet.

Around the same time, French actress [Brigitte Bardot](/wiki/Brigitte_Bardot "Brigitte Bardot") was given the opportunity to cast her own leading man in her next movie after her success in [Roger Vadim](/wiki/Roger_Vadim "Roger Vadim")'s *[And God Created Woman](/wiki/And_God_Created_Woman_(1956_film) "And God Created Woman (1956 film)")*, and she chose Boyd.[[16]](#cite_note-16) From August to October 1957,[[17]](#cite_note-17) Bardot, Boyd, and [Alida Valli](/wiki/Alida_Valli "Alida Valli") filmed the lusty romance *[The Night Heaven Fell](/wiki/The_Night_Heaven_Fell "The Night Heaven Fell")*, directed by [Roger Vadim](/wiki/Roger_Vadim "Roger Vadim") in Paris and in the region of [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga"), Spain, specifically the small, whitewashed town of Mijas.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Being in the Bardot spotlight added much to Boyd's film credit, in addition to bringing him notice in Hollywood.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Boyd finally arrived in Hollywood in January 1958 to take on his first true Hollywood role as the leader of a quartet of renegade outlaws in the Twentieth Century Fox western *[The Bravados](/wiki/The_Bravados "The Bravados")*, which starred [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck") and [Joan Collins](/wiki/Joan_Collins "Joan Collins"). Even though this was a Hollywood production, the actual filming took place in [Morelia](/wiki/Morelia "Morelia"), Mexico.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### *Ben-Hur*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ben-Hur")]

After the filming of *[The Bravados](/wiki/The_Bravados "The Bravados")* was complete in late March 1958, Boyd returned to Hollywood to audition for the role of Messala in [MGM](/wiki/MGM "MGM")'s upcoming epic *[Ben-Hur](/wiki/Ben-Hur_(1959_film) "Ben-Hur (1959 film)")*. Many other actors, including [Victor Mature](/wiki/Victor_Mature "Victor Mature"), [Kirk Douglas](/wiki/Kirk_Douglas "Kirk Douglas"), [Leslie Nielsen](/wiki/Leslie_Nielsen "Leslie Nielsen") and [Stewart Granger](/wiki/Stewart_Granger "Stewart Granger") had been considered for the part,[[21]](#cite_note-21) but Boyd's screen test convinced director [William Wyler](/wiki/William_Wyler "William Wyler") that he had found the perfect villain for his epic. Wyler had also admired Boyd's performance in *[The Man Who Never Was](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Never_Was "The Man Who Never Was")* the previous year. Boyd was hurried off to join lead actor [Charlton Heston](/wiki/Charlton_Heston "Charlton Heston") in Rome in May 1958 to learn the chariot racing aspect of his role. Heston had already been practising behind the chariot for weeks, so Boyd needed to learn quickly. Boyd was also required to wear brown contact lenses as Messala, which irritated his eyes and caused vision problems for a few months after the movie was completed. Boyd described the filming experience of *Ben-Hur* as the most exciting experience of his life.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Years after the movie was released, interim *[Ben-Hur](/wiki/Ben-Hur_(1959_film) "Ben-Hur (1959 film)")* screenwriter and novelist [Gore Vidal](/wiki/Gore_Vidal "Gore Vidal") revealed that Boyd had portrayed his character Messala in *Ben-Hur* with an underlying homosexual energy, as instructed to by Vidal when he greets Judah Ben-Hur (Charlton Heston) in the opening sequence. In Vidal's autobiography *Palimpsest: A Memoir*[[23]](#cite_note-Gore_Vidal_pages_303-23) he described his discussion first with Wyler concerning Messala's underlying motivation, namely that Messala and Judah Ben-Hur had previously been lovers. This was based on an idea by Vidal to enhance the tension between the two main antagonists. Wyler specifically told Vidal, "You talk to Boyd. But don't *you* say a word to Chuck or he'll fall apart."[[24]](#cite_note-24) In *Palimpsest*, Vidal said, "Over the next few years, whenever we met [William Wyler], we quarreled amiably over what I had put in the scene and what Steven Boyd is clearly playing." Vidal later came into conflict with Heston about his version of the Messala/Ben-Hur relationship and the implications surrounding *Ben-Hur*.[[23]](#cite_note-Gore_Vidal_pages_303-23)

After the filming of *Ben-Hur* was completed Boyd returned to Hollywood in early 1959 to star with [Susan Hayward](/wiki/Susan_Hayward "Susan Hayward") in the Canadian-based drama *[Woman Obsessed](/wiki/Woman_Obsessed "Woman Obsessed")*. Some advertisements for this movie labelled Boyd as "The Young New Clark Gable".[[25]](#cite_note-blade-gable-25) He was then part of another ensemble cast in the adaptation of [Rona Jaffe](/wiki/Rona_Jaffe "Rona Jaffe")'s novel *[The Best of Everything](/wiki/The_Best_of_Everything_(film) "The Best of Everything (film)")*, filmed in May and June 1959 at Fox Studios in Hollywood and on location in New York City.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Stephen_Boyd_in_Ben_Hur_trailer.jpg/250px-Stephen_Boyd_in_Ben_Hur_trailer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stephen_Boyd_in_Ben_Hur_trailer.jpg)

From the trailer for the film *[Ben-Hur](/wiki/Ben-Hur_(1959_film) "Ben-Hur (1959 film)")* (1959)

*Ben-Hur* was released in November 1959. His portrayal of Messala was praised by critics. Press columnist Erskine Johnson wrote, "A brass hat and the armor of a Roman warrior in *Ben-Hur* does for Stephen Boyd what a tight dress does for Marilyn Monroe."[[26]](#cite_note-26) Ruth Waterbury, in her Boyd feature in the *Pittsburgh Post-Gazette*, described Boyd's character as "the dangerously masculine and quite magnificent Messala."[[27]](#cite_note-27) *Modern Screen* magazine in 1960 stated that Boyd's ruthless Messala had "lost the chariot race but captured the sympathy and sex appeal of *Ben-Hur*."[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1960s")]

Boyd was featured in the TV programme *[This Is Your Life](/wiki/This_Is_Your_Life_(American_franchise) "This Is Your Life (American franchise)")* on 3 February 1960, which featured many of Boyd's family members and acquaintances (including Michael Redgrave) telling stories about his early life and film career.[[29]](#cite_note-29) He was being sent dozens of starring roles, most of which he had to turn down due to other obligations. He opted out of the biblical epic *[The Story of Ruth](/wiki/The_Story_of_Ruth "The Story of Ruth")*, which didn't please Fox studios, and he was one of the front-runners to star with [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe") in her picture *[Let's Make Love](/wiki/Let%27s_Make_Love "Let's Make Love")*.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In early 1960, Boyd won the [Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture") for his performance in *Ben-Hur*.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In January 1960, Boyd made a guest appearance alongside the silent-era *Ben-Hur* stars [Ramon Novarro](/wiki/Ramon_Novarro "Ramon Novarro") and [Francis X. Bushman](/wiki/Francis_X._Bushman "Francis X. Bushman") on [Hedda Hopper](/wiki/Hedda_Hopper "Hedda Hopper")'s television programme *Hedda Hopper's Hollywood*.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In February 1960, he starred in the [Playhouse 90](/wiki/Playhouse_90 "Playhouse 90") television performance called *The Sound of Trumpets* with [Dolores Hart](/wiki/Dolores_Hart "Dolores Hart"), which garnered good reviews. He also appeared as a singing guest on [The Dinah Shore Chevy Show](/wiki/The_Dinah_Shore_Chevy_Show "The Dinah Shore Chevy Show") on 13 March 1960 where he performed two Irish folk songs with Shore, "The Leprechaun Song" [[33]](#cite_note-33) and "Molly Malone", and an Irish step dance.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Boyd's next choice was *[The Big Gamble](/wiki/The_Big_Gamble_(1961_film) "The Big Gamble (1961 film)")*, which featured [Juliette Gréco](/wiki/Juliette_Gr%C3%A9co "Juliette Gréco"). It was filmed on the [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast "Ivory Coast") of West Africa, [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), and southern France in the spring and summer of 1961.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) The crew slept in tents in the jungle that were guarded by natives on parole for [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Boyd was originally chosen to play [Mark Antony](/wiki/Mark_Antony "Mark Antony") opposite [Elizabeth Taylor](/wiki/Elizabeth_Taylor "Elizabeth Taylor") in 20th Century Fox's production of *[Cleopatra](/wiki/Cleopatra_(1963_film) "Cleopatra (1963 film)")* (1963) under the direction of [Rouben Mamoulian](/wiki/Rouben_Mamoulian "Rouben Mamoulian"). He began film work in September 1960 but eventually withdrew after Elizabeth Taylor's severe illness postponed the film for months.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

After several months without active work, Boyd got his first post-*Cleopatra* role.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The film was *[The Inspector](/wiki/The_Inspector_(1962_film) "The Inspector (1962 film)")*, re-titled *Lisa* for the American release. It was based on the novel by [Jan de Hartog](/wiki/Jan_de_Hartog "Jan de Hartog") and co-starred actress [Dolores Hart](/wiki/Dolores_Hart "Dolores Hart"). The film was made in Amsterdam, London and Wales during the summer of 1961. On 9 January 1962, Boyd was featured in a television film from [General Electric Theater](/wiki/General_Electric_Theater "General Electric Theater") called *The Wall Between*, co-starring [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") and [Gloria Talbott](/wiki/Gloria_Talbott "Gloria Talbott").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Boyd flew to Rome in the summer of 1962 to act with [Gina Lollobrigida](/wiki/Gina_Lollobrigida "Gina Lollobrigida") in *[Imperial Venus](/wiki/Imperial_Venus_(film) "Imperial Venus (film)")*, a romantic epic about the many loves of [Pauline Bonaparte](/wiki/Pauline_Bonaparte "Pauline Bonaparte"), the sister of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"). This film was the first film to be banned by the [Motion Picture Association of America](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Association_of_America "Motion Picture Association of America") for male nudity. Boyd appeared in a humorous bedroom scene, naked with only his lower half covered by a bed-sheet.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The suggestion of nudity was too much for the censors and the movie was never released in the United States.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Boyd returned to the States briefly after finishing *Imperial Venus*, where he appeared for the second time on [The Dinah Shore Chevy Show](/wiki/The_Dinah_Shore_Chevy_Show "The Dinah Shore Chevy Show"), which aired on 11 November 1962. For this program Boyd was a last-minute replacement for actor [James Garner](/wiki/James_Garner "James Garner") and joined Shore and entertainer [Dean Martin](/wiki/Dean_Martin "Dean Martin") for a few musical numbers.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Boyd arrived in Spain in early 1963 to begin work on [Samuel Bronston](/wiki/Samuel_Bronston "Samuel Bronston")'s production of *[The Fall of the Roman Empire](/wiki/The_Fall_of_the_Roman_Empire_(film) "The Fall of the Roman Empire (film)")*, directed by [Anthony Mann](/wiki/Anthony_Mann "Anthony Mann"). This was filmed during a severely cold winter in Europe and the production in the [Sierra de Guadarrama](/wiki/Sierra_de_Guadarrama "Sierra de Guadarrama") of Spain encountered several challenges with the snow.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Boyd's co-star was another Italian legend, [Sophia Loren](/wiki/Sophia_Loren "Sophia Loren"). Boyd also had the opportunity to ride another chariot in this film. Although the movie did well internationally when it was released in April 1964, it was a box office failure in the United States and signalled the end of Roman epics in the 1960s. More appreciated with the passing of time, *[The Fall of the Roman Empire](/wiki/The_Fall_of_the_Roman_Empire_(film) "The Fall of the Roman Empire (film)")* was also recognised by critics as being a major inspiration for Ridley Scott's [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award")-winning movie *[Gladiator](/wiki/Gladiator_(2000_film) "Gladiator (2000 film)").*[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Boyd flew back to Hollywood in the summer to star in a *[Bob Hope Presents the Chrysler Theatre](/wiki/Bob_Hope_Presents_the_Chrysler_Theatre "Bob Hope Presents the Chrysler Theatre")* episode with [Louis Jourdan](/wiki/Louis_Jourdan "Louis Jourdan") called *[War of Nerves](/wiki/War_of_Nerves_(Bob_Hope_Presents_the_Chrysler_Theatre) "War of Nerves (Bob Hope Presents the Chrysler Theatre)")*, which aired on 3 January 1964.[[50]](#cite_note-50) He then returned to Europe to film the suspenseful *[The Third Secret](/wiki/The_Third_Secret_(film) "The Third Secret (film)")* starring [Pamela Franklin](/wiki/Pamela_Franklin "Pamela Franklin"), [Richard Attenborough](/wiki/Richard_Attenborough "Richard Attenborough"), [Jack Hawkins](/wiki/Jack_Hawkins "Jack Hawkins")
and [Diane Cilento](/wiki/Diane_Cilento "Diane Cilento").

On 23 December 1963, Boyd became a naturalised US citizen during a ceremony at the Federal Building in Los Angeles, California.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Boyd was originally cast as the lead in Anthony Mann's World War II drama *The Unknown Battle*, which was set to film in early 1964 with co-stars [Elke Sommer](/wiki/Elke_Sommer "Elke Sommer") and [Anthony Perkins](/wiki/Anthony_Perkins "Anthony Perkins") in Norway.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) After several weeks of waiting, studio funding for the project fell through.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Boyd sued Mann for $500,000 for a breach of contract, missed time and other lost film opportunities.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The project was completed by Mann a year later and released as *[Heroes of Telemark](/wiki/Heroes_of_Telemark "Heroes of Telemark")* with [Kirk Douglas](/wiki/Kirk_Douglas "Kirk Douglas") replacing Boyd as the lead.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In 1964, Boyd continued to make films in Europe, travelling to [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") to star as the villain Jamuga in the epic *[Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan_(1965_film) "Genghis Khan (1965 film)")*. Boyd was the top billed and therefore the top paid star in the epic, and this apparently caused friction with up-and-coming star [Omar Sharif](/wiki/Omar_Sharif "Omar Sharif").[[57]](#cite_note-57) After completing *Genghis Khan*, Boyd trekked to [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") for a brief appearance as the regal King [Nimrod](/wiki/Nimrod "Nimrod") at [The Tower of Babel](/wiki/The_Tower_of_Babel "The Tower of Babel") in [Dino de Laurentiis](/wiki/Dino_de_Laurentiis "Dino de Laurentiis")'s production of *[The Bible: In the Beginning...](/wiki/The_Bible:_In_the_Beginning... "The Bible: In the Beginning...")*, directed by [John Huston](/wiki/John_Huston "John Huston").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Boyd returned to the United States to start work on the Twentieth Century Fox science fiction adventure *[Fantastic Voyage](/wiki/Fantastic_Voyage "Fantastic Voyage")*.[[59]](#cite_note-59) This was filmed in the early part of 1965. In the summer of 1965, Boyd joined German star [Elke Sommer](/wiki/Elke_Sommer "Elke Sommer") and music legend [Tony Bennett](/wiki/Tony_Bennett "Tony Bennett") to film the Hollywood drama *[The Oscar](/wiki/The_Oscar_(film) "The Oscar (film)")*, based on the eponymous [Richard Sale](/wiki/Richard_Sale_(director) "Richard Sale (director)") novel. The movie was a popular success, but maligned by film critics.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Boyd made a 10-day visit to [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") in December 1965 to film his scenes for the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") film project [Terence Young](/wiki/Terence_Young_(director) "Terence Young (director)")'s *[The Poppy Is Also a Flower](/wiki/The_Poppy_Is_Also_a_Flower "The Poppy Is Also a Flower")*,[[61]](#cite_note-61) written by [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") creator [Ian Fleming](/wiki/Ian_Fleming "Ian Fleming").

In 1966, [Joseph Levine](/wiki/Joseph_E._Levine "Joseph E. Levine") hired Boyd for his film project, *[The Caper of the Golden Bulls](/wiki/The_Caper_of_the_Golden_Bulls "The Caper of the Golden Bulls")*, based on a [William McGivern](/wiki/William_McGivern "William McGivern") novel. This movie was partly filmed on location in Spain in the summer of 1966.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Boyd starred in the spy thriller *[Assignment K](/wiki/Assignment_K "Assignment K")* with [Camilla Sparv](/wiki/Camilla_Sparv "Camilla Sparv"), which was filmed in Germany, Austria and London during February and March 1967.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Boyd grew a full beard [[64]](#cite_note-64) for his next role as the iconic Irish playwright and critic [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw") in the [Off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Broadway "Off-Broadway") play called *The Bashful Genius* written by Harold Callen. This was Boyd's first return to the stage since the mid-1950s, and the experience for Boyd was immensely rewarding on a personal level.[[65]](#cite_note-65) He received excellent reviews for his nuanced performance of the multi-faceted Shaw.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The play had a very brief run during the summer of 1967 in [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and [Falmouth, Massachusetts](/wiki/Falmouth,_Massachusetts "Falmouth, Massachusetts").[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In early 1968, Boyd was cast as the villain opposite [Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery") and [Brigitte Bardot](/wiki/Brigitte_Bardot "Brigitte Bardot") in the western adventure *[*Shalako*](/wiki/Shalako_(film) "Shalako (film)")*, based on the [Louis L'Amour](/wiki/Louis_L%27Amour "Louis L'Amour") novel.[[69]](#cite_note-69) *Shalako* was filmed in the early part of 1968 in [Almería](/wiki/Almer%C3%ADa "Almería"), Spain. After returning to the United States, Boyd took the role of the cruel slave master Nathan MacKay in the Southern drama *[Slaves](/wiki/Slaves_(film) "Slaves (film)")*, also starring [Ossie Davis](/wiki/Ossie_Davis "Ossie Davis") and singer [Dionne Warwick](/wiki/Dionne_Warwick "Dionne Warwick"). The film was loosely based on the [Harriet Beecher Stowe](/wiki/Harriet_Beecher_Stowe "Harriet Beecher Stowe") novel *[Uncle Tom's Cabin](/wiki/Uncle_Tom%27s_Cabin "Uncle Tom's Cabin")*. It was filmed during the summer of 1968 near [Shreveport, Louisiana](/wiki/Shreveport,_Louisiana "Shreveport, Louisiana").[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) The film was released during the volatile civil rights era and in May 1969 Boyd attended the premiere alongside Dionne Warwick in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore")[[72]](#cite_note-72)

It was around this time that Boyd began his interest in [L. Ron Hubbard](/wiki/L._Ron_Hubbard "L. Ron Hubbard")'s Church of [Scientology](/wiki/Scientology "Scientology"), which made him one of the first Hollywood stars to be involved in it.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Boyd had always expressed an interest in esoteric religions.[[74]](#cite_note-Loner-74) In an interview in August 1969 with the *Detroit Free Press*, Boyd explained that Scientology had helped him through the filming of *Slaves*, and that to him Scientology was "a process used to make you capable of learning. Scientology is nothing. It means only what you want it to. It is not a church you go to pray, but a church that you go to learn. It is no good unless you apply it. It is the application."[[75]](#cite_note-75) Boyd apparently had been elevated to a Scientology Status of OT 6, a position above that of [Clear](/wiki/Clear_(Scientology) "Clear (Scientology)"). Boyd starred in and narrated a Scientology recruiting movie titled *Freedom* in 1970.[[76]](#cite_note-76) [Dolores Hart](/wiki/Dolores_Hart "Dolores Hart") wrote her friendship with Boyd was hurt by his scientology.[[77]](#cite_note-hart-77)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1970s")]

During the 1970s, the demand for Boyd in Hollywood diminished, so he focused his attention on European films and several television pilots and shows.[[78]](#cite_note-78) He made three films in Spain with director [José Antonio Nieves Conde](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Antonio_Nieves_Conde "José Antonio Nieves Conde"), including *[Marta](/wiki/Marta_(1971_film) "Marta (1971 film)")* in 1970, *[The Great Swindle](/wiki/The_Great_Swindle_(1971_film) "The Great Swindle (1971 film)")* in 1971, and *Casa Manchada* in 1975. He worked with cult director [Romain Gary](/wiki/Romain_Gary "Romain Gary") in the drug thriller *Kill!* in 1971. He also made several Westerns, including *[Hannie Caulder](/wiki/Hannie_Caulder "Hannie Caulder")* with Raquel Welch in 1971, *[The Man Called Noon](/wiki/The_Man_Called_Noon "The Man Called Noon")* in 1973, *[Those Dirty Dogs](/wiki/Those_Dirty_Dogs "Those Dirty Dogs")* in 1973, and *[Montana Trap](/wiki/Montana_Trap "Montana Trap") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potato_Fritz_(Film) "de:Potato Fritz (Film)")]* in 1976. Boyd continued to travel to a wide variety of locations to work, including Australia for *[The Hands of Cormac Joyce](/wiki/The_Hands_of_Cormac_Joyce "The Hands of Cormac Joyce")* in 1972, South Africa for *Control Factor* and *The Manipulator* in 1972–1973, [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") for the scuba diving adventure *[The Treasure of Jamaica Reef](/wiki/The_Treasure_of_Jamaica_Reef "The Treasure of Jamaica Reef")* in 1972, Florida for the television pilot *[Key West](/wiki/Key_West "Key West")* in 1973, and Hawaii in his last acting stint as a guest star on the popular television show *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")* in 1977. The episode *Up the Rebels* was the premiere episode of *Hawaii Five-O*'s tenth season, and it aired after Boyd's death on 15 September 1977. His most critically acclaimed role during the 1970s was as a colourful Irish gangster in the UK crime thriller *[The Squeeze](/wiki/The_Squeeze_(1977_film) "The Squeeze (1977 film)")* in 1977.

A letter from film producer [Euan Lloyd](/wiki/Euan_Lloyd "Euan Lloyd") (who produced such films as *Shalako*, *The Man Called Noon* and *The Wild Geese*), states that "Stephen Boyd was one of the nicest, kindest people I have met in my lifetime, rare in this profession."[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Although Boyd spent most of his adult life travelling abroad for film work, he made his permanent home in southern [California](/wiki/California "California"). At one point in the 1960s, he had three homes there — one above the Sunset Strip, one in [Tarzana](/wiki/Tarzana,_Los_Angeles "Tarzana, Los Angeles") and another in [Palm Springs](/wiki/Palm_Springs "Palm Springs"), where he enjoyed his favorite pastime, golf.[[74]](#cite_note-Loner-74)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Boyd was first married in 1958 to Italian-born MCA executive Mariella Di Sarzana during the filming of *Ben-Hur*. They separated after just three weeks. Concerning his short-lived marriage to Sarzana, Boyd explained: "It was my fault. I'm an Irish so-and-so when I'm working. I hadn't been married a week when we both knew we had made a mistake. She is a nice girl but we were just not meant for each other. I suppose I wasn't ready for marriage. Maybe I was still too much of an adolescent."[[80]](#cite_note-80) They officially divorced in early 1959.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

He had a friendship with [Brigitte Bardot](/wiki/Brigitte_Bardot "Brigitte Bardot") with whom he starred in two movies.[[82]](#cite_note-Photoplay_Film_1968-82) It even caused Brigitte's husband at the time, [Gunter Sachs](/wiki/Gunter_Sachs "Gunter Sachs"), to ask for a divorce.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Dolores_Hart_en_Stephen_Boyd_%281961%29.jpg/250px-Dolores_Hart_en_Stephen_Boyd_%281961%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dolores_Hart_en_Stephen_Boyd_(1961).jpg)

Hart and Boyd in 1961

Boyd also had a close relationship with actress [Dolores Hart](/wiki/Dolores_Hart "Dolores Hart") who describes what was her only romance with a co-star in her autobiography *The Ear of the Heart*.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Boyd eventually rejected her advances, but they remained close friends even after she turned to the cloistered life of a nun in 1963. He visited her in 1966 at the Abbey of Regina Laudis in Connecticut and remained in communication with her until the early 1970s.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Boyd had an affair that seems to have been with [Marisa Mell](/wiki/Marisa_Mell "Marisa Mell").[[86]](#cite_note-86) They met while filming the movie *[Marta](/wiki/Marta_(1971_film) "Marta (1971 film)")* in 1970. During the second film they made together, *[The Great Swindle](/wiki/The_Great_Swindle_(1971_film) "The Great Swindle (1971 film)")*, the two became lovers.[[87]](#cite_note-Mell,_Marisa._Coverlove,_1990-87)[[88]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-88) In early 1972, after Boyd broke off the affair, Mell said: "We both believe in reincarnation, and we realized we've already been lovers in three different lifetimes, and in each one I made him suffer terribly."[[89]](#cite_note-89) Mell remembered Boyd many years later in her autobiography *Cover Love* from 1990, dedicating a chapter to their affair.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Boyd's last marriage took place in 1974 to Elizabeth Mills,[[91]](#cite_note-91) a secretary at the British Arts Council, whom he had known since 1953. Mills followed Boyd to the United States in the late 1950s and was his personal assistant, friend and confidante for many years before marrying him in the mid-1970s.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Death")]

Boyd died of a massive [heart attack](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction") on 2 June 1977 at the age of 45 while playing golf with his wife, Elizabeth Mills, at the Porter Valley Country Club in [Northridge, California](/wiki/Northridge,_Los_Angeles "Northridge, Los Angeles").[[93]](#cite_note-93) He was in talks to play the role of the [Regimental Sergeant Major](/wiki/Regimental_sergeant_major "Regimental sergeant major") in [Euan Lloyd](/wiki/Euan_Lloyd "Euan Lloyd")'s *[The Wild Geese](/wiki/The_Wild_Geese "The Wild Geese")* before his death.[[94]](#cite_note-euanlloyd-94)

He was cremated and his ashes were interred in [Oakwood Memorial Park Cemetery](/wiki/Oakwood_Memorial_Park_Cemetery "Oakwood Memorial Park Cemetery") in [Chatsworth, California](/wiki/Chatsworth,_Los_Angeles "Chatsworth, Los Angeles"). Mills was interred with him at the time of her death in 2007. He is also remembered on his parents' grave in the Clandeboye Cemetery, [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_County_Down "Bangor, County Down"), [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland").[[4]](#cite_note-newsletter.co.uk-4)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stephen_Boyd&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legacy")]

On 4 July 2018, the Ulster History Circle, a voluntary organisation which erects plaques across the province of Ulster to celebrate people of achievement, commemorated Stephen Boyd with a [blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque#Northern_Ireland_and_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Blue plaque") close to his birthplace [[95]](#cite_note-95) at 'Moygara', Shore Road, Whitehouse ([Newtownabbey](/wiki/Newtownabbey "Newtownabbey"), [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland")).[[96]](#cite_note-96)
